From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

American professional Negro League baseball team (1936–1950)

| Newark Eagles | |
| --- | --- |
| Information | |
| League | * Independent (1933) * [Negro National League (II)](/wiki/Negro_National_League_(1933%E2%80%931948) "Negro National League (1933–1948)") (1934–1948) * [Negro American League](/wiki/Negro_American_League "Negro American League") (1949–1951) |
| Location | [Newark, New Jersey](/wiki/Newark,_New_Jersey "Newark, New Jersey") |
| [Ballpark](/wiki/Ballpark "Ballpark") | * General Electric Field ([Bloomfield, New Jersey](/wiki/Bloomfield,_New_Jersey "Bloomfield, New Jersey")) (1934 Dodgers)[[1]](#cite_note-1) * Ollemar Stadium ([Irvington, New Jersey](/wiki/Irvington,_New_Jersey "Irvington, New Jersey")) (1935 Dodgers)[[2]](#cite_note-2) * [Ebbets Field](/wiki/Ebbets_Field "Ebbets Field") (Brooklyn) (1935 Eagles)[[3]](#cite_note-3) * [Ruppert Stadium](/wiki/Ruppert_Stadium_(Newark) "Ruppert Stadium (Newark)") (Newark) (1936–1948) * [Buffalo Stadium](/wiki/Buffalo_Stadium "Buffalo Stadium") (Houston) (1949–1950) |
| Established | [1933](/wiki/1933_in_baseball "1933 in baseball") (est. 1936 through merger) |
| Disbanded | [1950](/wiki/1950_in_baseball "1950 in baseball") |
| Nicknames | * Newark Dodgers\* (1933–1935) * Brooklyn Eagles\* (1935) * \*merged 1935 * Newark Eagles (1936–1948) * Houston Eagles (1949–1950) * New Orleans Eagles (1951) |
| Negro World Series championships | [1946](/wiki/1946_Negro_World_Series "1946 Negro World Series") |
| League titles | [1946](/wiki/1946_Negro_World_Series "1946 Negro World Series") |

The **Newark Eagles** were a professional [Negro league baseball](/wiki/Negro_league_baseball "Negro league baseball") team which played in the [Negro National League](/wiki/Negro_National_League_(1933%E2%80%931948) "Negro National League (1933–1948)") from [1936](/wiki/1936_in_sports "1936 in sports") to [1948](/wiki/1936_in_sports "1936 in sports"). They were owned by [Abe](/wiki/Abe_Manley "Abe Manley") and [Effa Manley](/wiki/Effa_Manley "Effa Manley").[[4]](#cite_note-4)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Newark_Eagles&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

For a list of annual win-loss records, see [List of Newark Eagles seasons](/wiki/List_of_Newark_Eagles_seasons "List of Newark Eagles seasons").

### Formation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Newark_Eagles&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Formation")]

The Newark Eagles were formed in 1936 when the **Newark Dodgers**, established in 1933, merged with the **Brooklyn Eagles**, established in 1935. [Abe Manley](/wiki/Abe_Manley "Abe Manley") and his wife [Effa Manley](/wiki/Effa_Manley "Effa Manley"), owners and founders of the Brooklyn Eagles, purchased the Newark Dodgers franchise and combined the teams' assets and player rosters.[[5]](#cite_note-NLBM-5) Charles Tyler, the previous owner of the Dodgers, signed the team over in exchange for cancellation of an approximately $500 debt that Tyler owed Abe Manley.[[6]](#cite_note-bbbb-6)

Team management was left to Effa, making the Eagles the third professional baseball team owned and operated by a woman. The first such team was the [St. Louis Cardinals](/wiki/St._Louis_Cardinals "St. Louis Cardinals"), which was owned by [Helene Hathaway Britton](/wiki/Helene_Hathaway_Britton "Helene Hathaway Britton") from 1911 to 1917, and the second such team was the [Indianapolis ABCs](/wiki/Indianapolis_ABCs "Indianapolis ABCs") who were owned by Olivia Taylor from 1922 to 1926.[[7]](#cite_note-7) The Eagles shared [Ruppert Stadium](/wiki/Ruppert_Stadium_(Newark) "Ruppert Stadium (Newark)") with the minor league [Newark Bears](/wiki/Newark_Bears_(International_League) "Newark Bears (International League)").

> The Eagles were to (black) [Newark](/wiki/Newark,_New_Jersey "Newark, New Jersey") what the Dodgers were to Brooklyn.

— Eagles star [Max Manning](/wiki/Max_Manning "Max Manning")

### Decline and demise

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Newark_Eagles&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Decline and demise")]

After the close of the 1948 season, in the aftermath of [Jackie Robinson](/wiki/Jackie_Robinson "Jackie Robinson")'s successful [integration](/wiki/Baseball_color_line "Baseball color line") of [Major League Baseball](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball "Major League Baseball") a year earlier, the [Negro National League](/wiki/Negro_National_League_(1933%E2%80%931948) "Negro National League (1933–1948)") contracted and merged into the [Negro American League](/wiki/Negro_American_League "Negro American League"). The Eagles were sold and moved to [Houston, Texas](/wiki/Houston,_Texas "Houston, Texas") for the 1949 season,[[5]](#cite_note-NLBM-5) where they became known as the **Houston Eagles**, part of the NAL's western division. Two years later they again relocated, this time to New Orleans. The **New Orleans Eagles** lasted one year before folding after the 1951 season.[[8]](#cite_note-8): 5

## Negro World Series champions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Newark_Eagles&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Negro World Series champions")]

Under Effa Manley's guidance, the [1946](/wiki/1946_in_baseball "1946 in baseball") team won the [Negro World Series](/wiki/Negro_World_Series "Negro World Series"), upsetting the [Kansas City Monarchs](/wiki/Kansas_City_Monarchs "Kansas City Monarchs") in a 7-game series.[[5]](#cite_note-NLBM-5)

## Players

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Newark_Eagles&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Players")]

For a more comprehensive list, see [Newark Eagles all-time roster](/wiki/Newark_Eagles_all-time_roster "Newark Eagles all-time roster").

### Notable alumni

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Newark_Eagles&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Notable alumni")]

* [Don Newcombe](/wiki/Don_Newcombe "Don Newcombe") (1944–1945) 4 x MLB All Star; 1949 Rookie of the year; 1956 [Cy Young Award](/wiki/Cy_Young_Award "Cy Young Award"); 1956 NL Most Valuable Player
